hero.tagline: Turning pixels and depth into something the robot can act on
hero.intro: Computer vision is the part of the robot that watches and understands. Given an image (or a depth map, or a point cloud), it decides 'there is a cup here, oriented like this, the door is open, the path is clear'. Everything downstream - planning, manipulation, navigation - depends on it being right. This branch walks the pipeline: how a camera projects 3D into 2D, classical image processing, deep object detection on a ROS topic, point clouds, sensor fusion, SLAM, and 6-DoF pose estimation. By the end you can build the perception layer of a real robot.

sections.heading: The perception stack
sections.body: Most robots run a pipeline that looks roughly like this: drivers publish raw sensor data → calibration aligns sensors with the robot's frames → low-level vision extracts features (edges, keypoints, depth) → mid-level vision groups them into objects and surfaces → a world model fuses everything over time. Each layer is a tradeoff between speed and richness - perception is almost always the bottleneck in a robot's control loop, so understanding what each layer costs is critical.
bullets: Drivers - the camera or LiDAR's ROS 2 node, publishing /image_raw, /points, /imu/data
Calibration - intrinsic (the camera itself) and extrinsic (camera ↔ robot) transforms
Detection / segmentation - what is in the scene? YOLO, SAM, Mask R-CNN
3D reasoning - point clouds (PCL, Open3D), depth fusion, pose estimation
World model - TF tree, octomap, scene graph, or a custom representation
Fusion - Kalman / particle filters merging multiple sensors over time

sections.heading: Camera fundamentals: pinhole model + intrinsics
sections.body:
Every perception pipeline starts with the pinhole camera model. A 3D point in the camera frame [X, Y, Z] projects to a 2D pixel [u, v] via the intrinsic matrix K. Calibration determines K - focal lengths fx, fy and the principal point (cx, cy). Without good calibration, every depth measurement and every back-projection is wrong.

Distortion (barrel, pincushion) is modeled with extra parameters (k1, k2, p1, p2, k3 for radial + tangential). cv::undistort or image_proc handles this for you once calibrated.
bullets: Intrinsics K = [[fx, 0, cx], [0, fy, cy], [0, 0, 1]] - describes the camera itself
Extrinsics [R | t] - the rigid transform from world frame to camera frame
Distortion coefficients - fix lens warping before doing geometry
Calibrate with a checkerboard or charuco target; ROS has tools for both

codeBlocks.title: Calibrate a monocular camera with the ROS 2 tool
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 run camera_calibration cameracalibrator \
    --size 8x6 --square 0.025 \
    --ros-args -r image:=/camera/image_raw -r camera:=/camera
codeBlocks.note: Print a checkerboard, point the camera at it, move it through 9+ poses.

codeBlocks.title: Pixel (u, v) + depth → 3D point in camera frame

sections.heading: Bring up an Intel RealSense camera
sections.body: RealSense is the workhorse stereo + RGB-D camera in academic robotics. Here's the full path from clone to streaming topics in your workspace.

codeBlocks.title: Create your workspace and clone the driver
Install dependencies and build
Source and launch with depth alignment
codeBlocks.code:
source ~/{{ws}}/install/setup.bash
ros2 launch realsense2_camera rs_launch.py \
    enable_color:=true enable_depth:=true \
    align_depth.enable:=true pointcloud.enable:=true

sections.heading: Classical image processing with OpenCV
sections.body: Before reaching for deep learning, classical CV solves a surprising number of robot perception problems - color thresholding for sorting, edge detection for line following, blob detection for fiducials. Knowing OpenCV well makes you faster.

codeBlocks.title: Color-space thresholding to find a red object
codeBlocks.code:
import cv2
import numpy as np

def find_red(bgr_image):
    hsv = cv2.cvtColor(bgr_image, cv2.COLOR_BGR2HSV)
    # Red wraps around hue 0/180 - use two ranges
    m1 = cv2.inRange(hsv, (0,   100, 100), (10,  255, 255))
    m2 = cv2.inRange(hsv, (170, 100, 100), (180, 255, 255))
    mask = cv2.bitwise_or(m1, m2)
    mask = cv2.morphologyEx(mask, cv2.MORPH_OPEN,
                            np.ones((5, 5), np.uint8))
    contours, _ = cv2.findContours(mask, cv2.RETR_EXTERNAL,
                                   cv2.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)
    if not contours: return None
    c = max(contours, key=cv2.contourArea)
    M = cv2.moments(c)
    return (int(M['m10']/M['m00']), int(M['m01']/M['m00']))

codeBlocks.title: Detect ArUco fiducials (great for known objects)
codeBlocks.code:
import cv2
import cv2.aruco as aruco

detector = aruco.ArucoDetector(
    aruco.getPredefinedDictionary(aruco.DICT_5X5_50),
    aruco.DetectorParameters())

def detect(frame):
    corners, ids, _ = detector.detectMarkers(frame)
    return ids, corners

sections.heading: Deep object detection on a ROS 2 topic
sections.body: Subscribe to /image_raw, run a model, republish detections. The 2026 model landscape: YOLOv10 / YOLOv11 (Ultralytics) remain the production default for closed-vocabulary detection (you know the classes up front). RT-DETR (Baidu) matches YOLO speed with better accuracy and is transformer-native. For open-vocabulary detection where you describe targets via text ('the red mug', 'a person holding a wrench'), Grounding DINO and OWL-ViT are the references - slower but unlock VLA-style prompted perception. For instance segmentation, SAM 2 (Meta, 2024) made promptable per-pixel masking a one-liner. The skeleton below is minimal so you can see the wiring; swap the model.

codeBlocks.title: yolo_detector_node.py
codeBlocks.code:
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from sensor_msgs.msg import Image
from vision_msgs.msg import Detection2DArray, Detection2D, ObjectHypothesisWithPose
from cv_bridge import CvBridge
from ultralytics import YOLO

class YoloDetector(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('yolo_detector')
        self.bridge = CvBridge()
        self.model = YOLO('yolov8n.pt')
        self.sub = self.create_subscription(
            Image, '/camera/camera/color/image_raw', self.cb, 10)
        self.pub = self.create_publisher(
            Detection2DArray, '/detections', 10)

    def cb(self, msg: Image):
        frame = self.bridge.imgmsg_to_cv2(msg, 'bgr8')
        results = self.model(frame, verbose=False)[0]
        out = Detection2DArray(header=msg.header)
        for box in results.boxes:
            d = Detection2D()
            d.bbox.center.position.x = float(box.xywh[0][0])
            d.bbox.center.position.y = float(box.xywh[0][1])
            d.bbox.size_x = float(box.xywh[0][2])
            d.bbox.size_y = float(box.xywh[0][3])
            h = ObjectHypothesisWithPose()
            h.hypothesis.class_id = self.model.names[int(box.cls)]
            h.hypothesis.score = float(box.conf)
            d.results.append(h)
            out.detections.append(d)
        self.pub.publish(out)

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    rclpy.spin(YoloDetector())

codeBlocks.title: Install + run
codeBlocks.code:
pip install ultralytics
cd ~/{{ws}}
colcon build --packages-select my_perception
source install/setup.bash
ros2 run my_perception yolo_detector_node

sections.heading: Point clouds - PCL + Open3D
sections.body: When you have depth, you get a point cloud - an unordered set of 3D points. Common operations: downsampling (voxel grid), filtering outliers, segmenting planes (RANSAC), clustering objects (Euclidean / DBSCAN), and registration (ICP). PCL is the C++ heavyweight, Open3D is the friendlier Python option.

codeBlocks.title: Voxel downsample + plane segmentation in Open3D
codeBlocks.code:
import open3d as o3d

pcd = o3d.io.read_point_cloud('scene.pcd')
pcd = pcd.voxel_down_sample(voxel_size=0.01)

# Find the dominant plane (table top)
plane_model, inliers = pcd.segment_plane(
    distance_threshold=0.01, ransac_n=3, num_iterations=1000)
table = pcd.select_by_index(inliers)
objects = pcd.select_by_index(inliers, invert=True)

# Cluster everything that's not the table
labels = objects.cluster_dbscan(eps=0.02, min_points=20)

codeBlocks.title: ICP - register a model cloud to a sensor cloud
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np
import open3d as o3d

result = o3d.pipelines.registration.registration_icp(
    source=model_pcd, target=sensor_pcd,
    max_correspondence_distance=0.02,
    init=np.eye(4),
    estimation_method=o3d.pipelines.registration
        .TransformationEstimationPointToPoint())
print(result.transformation)  # 4x4 pose of model in sensor frame

sections.heading: Visual SLAM - building maps from cameras
sections.body: When you don't have a pre-made map, run SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping). Visual SLAM uses cameras (mono, stereo, or RGB-D) instead of LiDAR. ORB-SLAM3 and RTAB-Map are the modern open-source baselines.
bullets: Sparse SLAM (ORB-SLAM3) - tracks keypoints, lightweight, sparse map
Dense SLAM (KinectFusion, BundleFusion) - voxel grids, full surface reconstruction
RGB-D SLAM (RTAB-Map) - pragmatic middle ground, ROS-native

codeBlocks.title: RTAB-Map with a RealSense camera
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-rtabmap-ros
ros2 launch rtabmap_examples realsense_d435i_color.launch.py

sections.heading: Sensor fusion & state estimation
sections.body:
Real robots have noisy sensors and need to combine them. The Kalman filter (and its extended/unscented variants) is the foundational algorithm - predict the next state from a motion model, correct it with sensor measurements, weighted by their uncertainties.

For robotics in ROS 2, robot_localization fuses any number of pose / IMU / odometry inputs into a single TF-published estimate without you writing the math.

codeBlocks.title: ekf.yaml - fuse wheel odom + IMU into /odometry/filtered
codeBlocks.code:
ekf_filter_node:
  ros__parameters:
    frequency: 30.0
    two_d_mode: true
    publish_tf: true
    odom_frame: odom
    base_link_frame: base_link
    world_frame: odom

    odom0: /wheel/odometry
    odom0_config: [true,  true,  false,
                   false, false, true,
                   true,  false, false,
                   false, false, true,
                   false, false, false]

    imu0: /imu/data
    imu0_config: [false, false, false,
                  true,  true,  true,
                  false, false, false,
                  true,  true,  true,
                  true,  true,  true]

codeBlocks.title: Launch the EKF
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-robot-localization
ros2 run robot_localization ekf_node \
    --ros-args --params-file ~/{{ws}}/config/ekf.yaml

sections.heading: Object tracking - keep an ID across frames
sections.body: Detection tells you what's in this frame; tracking tells you that the cup in frame N is the same cup in frame N+1. The trick is data association - given new detections and existing tracks, which goes with which. SORT solves this with a Kalman filter per track plus Hungarian-algorithm assignment. ByteTrack improves it by also matching low-confidence detections, which dramatically helps when objects are briefly occluded.
bullets: Kalman filter per track - predicts where each object will appear next frame
IoU + Hungarian - match detections to predictions by box overlap
ByteTrack: low-confidence detections often correspond to occluded real objects, don't throw them away
DeepSORT / StrongSORT - add a learned appearance embedding for re-identification across long gaps
For ROS 2: yolov8-ros and tracking_pkg wrap these on Detection2DArray topics

codeBlocks.title: Run ByteTrack alongside YOLO via Ultralytics
codeBlocks.code:
from ultralytics import YOLO

model = YOLO('yolov8n.pt')
# tracker = 'bytetrack.yaml' or 'botsort.yaml'
results = model.track(source='video.mp4', tracker='bytetrack.yaml',
                      persist=True, conf=0.25)
for r in results:
    if r.boxes.id is None: continue
    for box, tid in zip(r.boxes.xyxy, r.boxes.id):
        print(f'track {int(tid)} at {box.tolist()}')

sections.heading: Monocular depth estimation
sections.body: Single-camera depth used to be impossible without motion parallax. Modern transformers (MiDaS, ZoeDepth, Depth Anything V2) train on millions of mixed datasets and predict metric or relative depth from a single RGB image. Quality is now good enough for obstacle avoidance, AR, and rough scene understanding - though stereo + LiDAR still wins for safety-critical use.
bullets: MiDaS - relative depth, scale ambiguous, very robust
ZoeDepth - metric depth, fine-tuned on indoor / outdoor splits
Depth Anything V2 - current SOTA (2024), 25M parameters, runs on Jetson
Marigold - diffusion-based; slower but extremely sharp at edges
Use cases: AR overlay, obstacle proximity, rough 3D reconstruction
Caveat: scale drifts with lens / environment; calibrate against a known target before trusting metric output

codeBlocks.title: Depth Anything V2 inference
codeBlocks.code:
from transformers import pipeline
from PIL import Image

pipe = pipeline(task='depth-estimation',
                model='depth-anything/Depth-Anything-V2-Small-hf',
                device=0)
depth = pipe(Image.open('scene.jpg'))['depth']
depth.save('depth.png')

sections.heading: 6-DoF object pose estimation
sections.body: Knowing an object is in the scene (detection) is not enough for grasping - you need its full 6-DoF pose: position + orientation in the camera frame. Classic methods use known CAD models + ICP (PPF, Linemod). Modern learned methods (FoundationPose, PoseCNN, GDR-Net) generalize across objects without per-object training. FoundationPose (NVIDIA, 2024) sets the open-source bar - give it any RGB-D image and a CAD mesh, get the pose back.
bullets: Known model + ICP - classical baseline, accurate, slow, requires CAD
PoseCNN, GDR-Net - supervised CNNs, need per-object training data
FoundationPose - generalist, RGB-D + mesh, 2024 SOTA, ~50ms on a 4090
MegaPose - RGB-only, mesh-conditioned, harder to get production-grade
Output: 4×4 transform matrix, can drive MoveIt grasp planners directly
